item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it was about 1/2 shorter, I would upvote.

Then which half seems off? Let me hear you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know… General Winter SCP is cool, but there is something off with pacing. If it was about 1/2 shorter, I would upvote.

item.title: Re: Thanks, Attribution, Backstory and Other Matters
item.content:encoded: Updating this to include a link to Grandchildren Frost, a Secret Santa Art Exchange article penned by LordStonefish. The contents of Addendum 2617-9 are of his creation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Show Reasoning
Hide Reasoning

Crosslink was suggested by the author for the following reason:

I would imagine this to be something similar to SCP-2273, which ended up in Russia and this implied that it was applied as a weapon of the Russians. I suppose a crosslink can provide a cut Easter egg, since MacLeod (2273's author) originally planned for two instances (an American and a Russian) before only leaving the Russian instance as Alexei.

Editing this:

Resembles a powered exoskeleton of unknown origin, with design elements derivative of insects. Corresponds to no known weapons currently fielded by Russian Armed Force.

To this:

Resembles a powered exoskeleton of unknown origin, with design elements derivative of insects. Corresponds to no known weapons currently fielded by Russian Armed Force.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This should be higher rated. Very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay, GRU-P! Although I'm kinda sad that someone beat me to the idea of "General Winter" being an SCP…oh well. Very well written. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah, it's not that Ivan. Glad someone finally got the reference (or at least acknowledge it here). I watched that series and was influenced by it enough to throw in an easter egg.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interviewer: Agent Ivan Braginsky

Russia from Axis Powers Hetalia is a Foundation agent? You'd think he'd be a Keter-class skip…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The PPSh-41 will definitely be among the 2617-B instances, without a doubt. However, I have already included the PPD-40 and I don't think we need another Soviet submachine gun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Checking Wikipedia, you're correct. I've changed it to MP 40, which was used by Germany then. However, the Soviets did capture German equipment and used them during the Eastern Front nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A minor nitpick: the design work for the AK-47 didn't start until 1945, and it wasn't introduced until 1947. A bit anachronistic to have it appear in 1942. Otherwise, I liked it. Weapons firing ice spikes tickles me in just the right way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Has a few rough spots in the writing, but nothing awful. Upvoted for "It wishes to regain the favour of God, and we wish for protection. It's a mutual agreement."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap, this is literally General Winter, and that's so, so cool.

One niggling little problem, though- the use of the verb 'morphed' in the first addendum throws me right out of the 'Russian Legend' atmosphere you've got going. Feels anachronistic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm impressed by the historical depth of this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid not. "Alexei" refers to the real-life Tsarevich Alexei. In any case, "Alexei" is really Ivanoff who is deluded into becoming Alexei.

+ show block
– hide block

I originally wanted to use Anastasia as the CPU, as a reference to rumours of her surviving her family's murders.

But as I dig deeper, I've realised the following:

  • Salic law, so no women allowed on throne.
  • There were Alexei imposters too.
  • Why bother with real royalty when you can condition some random bloke into the role?

From my headcanon, the use of a Romanov imposter was an ideological victory for GRU-P as well. They basically cheated and warped the concept of "divine right" to their ends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is Alexei meant to be that Alexei? EDIT: I am dumb. Still, that'd be cool.

This is fucking awesome. I really love how all-in you went on this, making this a living and breathing thing to care about and how vibrant this skip is. I love the mention of Tsar's Seers in here, as well as GRU-P's role in the whole thing. Excellent job.

item.title: Thanks, Attribution, Backstory and Other Matters
item.content:encoded:
To start off, let me express thank you to all who had the pleasure (or displeasure) of reviewing this article in its various phases as a draft.

As for the pictures involved:

  • First picture (CC0) taken from Wikipedia (http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radio_masts_and_towers)
  • Second picture taken from 4kdordy under Creative Commons; changes made: Black and White. (https://www.flickr.com/photos/8593364@N06/2724376850/)

This article involves the following other article:

  • SCP-516 (Intelligent Tank) by ModernMajorGeneral

The Foundation precursor "Tsar's Seers" is credited to Agent MacLeod (mentioned in SCP-2237, which he plans to rewrite), although I took my personal liberties while writing this precursor.

+ show block
– hide block

So what actually happened?

The entity can only be summoned by an "absolutist Tsar", so GRU-P conditioned Ivanoff into the role of an absolute monarch. After "crowning" him, they incapacitate him and finished up on the machine.

P.S. They can't bring a real Romanov. That would cause quite a scandal (and purging).

So why was the place abandoned?

I would say, it was ultimately outdated. It could only be used against foreign armies within the USSR, and is useless for internal policing. Furthermore, I would imagine political implications over using an entity that vows allegiance to the Tsar.

But what happened to the Seers? Can the Tsar use the skip on his own?

The Tsar is necessary (or rather, his "divine right"), but the Seers know the mechanics behind it. From my headcanon, the Seers declined since the Napoleonic War with many members in exile or arrested by Russian authorities (they would be regarded lowly by the nobility). By WWI, the Seers were virtually no more save a few individuals who knew bits of the lore.
